title: Scene Illumination Guide
summary: Understand how area size, target footcandles, utilization, and light loss affect estimated lighting demand for a camera scene.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the lighting required to reach a target illumination level across a physical area after utilization and light-loss factors are considered.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when reviewing whether a camera scene is likely to have enough usable light for the desired image quality, especially at night or in poorly lit areas.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Area Width (ft)
items.description: The width of the scene area that needs illumination.
items.whyItMatters: Width is one side of the area calculation used to estimate total lighting demand.
items.commonMistake: Lighting the visible camera frame instead of the actual target area that needs detail.

items.label: Area Depth (ft)
items.description: The depth or length of the scene area that needs illumination.
items.whyItMatters: Depth combines with width to determine how much area must receive useful light.
items.commonMistake: Forgetting far-side areas where the camera still needs detail.

items.label: Target Footcandles (fc)
items.description: The desired illumination level across the target scene.
items.whyItMatters: The target footcandle level defines the lighting goal for the area.
items.commonMistake: Choosing a light level without considering the camera’s low-light performance and detail goal.

items.label: Utilization Factor (%)
items.description: The percentage of emitted light expected to reach and benefit the target area.
items.whyItMatters: Utilization accounts for fixture aim, spill, mounting, and how efficiently light reaches the scene.
items.commonMistake: Assuming every lumen produced by a fixture lands on the target area.

items.label: Light Loss Factor (%)
items.description: The percentage of lighting effectiveness retained after dirt, aging, lens loss, and maintenance conditions.
items.whyItMatters: Light loss prevents the design from relying on brand-new, clean, perfect fixture output forever.
items.commonMistake: Sizing lighting with no reserve for fixture aging, dirt, or maintenance.

links.label: Check whether detail target still fits
links.note: Good lighting supports recognition detail, but pixel count and angle still matter.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the lighting estimate appears practical for the target area. Watch means fixture layout, camera low-light behavior, or light-loss assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the scene may be underlit for the intended camera task.
bullets: Illumination math is only part of camera image quality.
Glare, backlight, shadows, IR behavior, and camera settings can change real performance.
Validate night views and motion performance before relying on the lighting plan.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
